questions.questionText: Which one of the following situations best reflects the term “Indirect Transfers” often talked about in the media with reference to India? out of its investment. investment. and transfers the proceeds to India.

options.a: An Indian company investing in a foreign enterprise and paying taxes to the foreign country on the profits arising
options.b: A foreign company investing in India and paying taxes to the country of its base on the profits arising out of its
options.c: An Indian company purchases tangible assets in a foreign country and sells such assets after their value increases
options.d: A foreign company transfers shares and such shares derive their substantial value from assets located in India.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**Indirect transfers refer to situations where foreign entities own shares or assets in India, when the shares of such **foreign entities are transferred and if such shares or interest derive a substantial value from assets located in **India. Then such transfer is commonly referred to as Indirect transfers. **The amendments made in the ITA in 2012 clarified that if a company is registered or incorporated outside India, its shares will be deemed to be or have always been situated in India if they derive their value substantially from the assets located in India. As a result, the persons who sold such shares of foreign companies before the enactment of the Act (i.e., May 28, 2012) also became liable to pay tax on the income earned from such sale.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to revised classification of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises in India (effective from 1st April
2025), consider the following statements:
I. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹1.5 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹8 crore.
II. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹4 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹8 crore.
III. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹1.8 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹12 crore.
How many of the above enterprises can be classified as a Micro Enterprise as per the revised official definition?
questions.questionText: With reference to revised classification of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises in India (effective from 1st April 2025), consider the following statements: I. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹1.5 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹8 crore. II. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹4 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹8 crore. III. An enterprise with Investment in Plant & Machinery of ₹1.8 crore and Annual Turnover of ₹12 crore. How many of the above enterprises can be classified as a Micro Enterprise as per the revised official definition?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**From April 1, 2025, the government has raised both investment and turnover limits for classifying MSMEs so that more units can benefit from the MSME framework and grow without quickly losing their status. For**micro enterprises**, the i**nvestment**ceiling in plant and machinery or equipment has been raised from ₹1 crore to**₹2.5 crore**, and**the **turnover limit**has been increased from ₹5 crore to**₹10 crore.**

Statement I is correct.**The enterprise with investment of ₹1.5 crore and annual turnover of ₹8 crore falls in the**Micro **category. **

Statement II is incorrect.**The enterprise with investment of ₹4 crore** does not qualify as a Micro Enterprise**and would be classified as a**Small Enterprise**. The investment threshold for a Micro Enterprise is ₹2.5 crore. Since this enterprise's Investment is**greater than the ₹**2.5 crore limit**, it falls under the **Small Enterprise** category.
**Statement III is incorrect.**The enterprise with **turnover of ₹**12 crore**does not qualify as a Micro Enterprise and would be classified as**a Small Enterprise**. The turnover threshold for a Micro Enterprise is ₹10 crore. Since this enterprise's Annual Turnover is greater than the ₹10 crore limit, it falls under the Small Enterprise category. Knowledge Base: For**small enterprises**, the**investment**threshold has gone up from ₹10 crore to**₹25 crore,**while the**turnover limit**has doubled from ₹50 crore to ₹**100 crore.**For**medium enterprises**, the i**nvestment **cap has been raised significantly from ₹50 crore to**₹125 crore,**and the**turnover ceiling**has been doubled from ₹250 crore to**₹500 **crore.**

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act 2003 provides for the responsibility of the Central
Government to ensure inter-generational equity in fiscal management.
Statement II: As per the Ricardian Equivalence, the government borrowings today will come at lesser cost than future borrowings to stimulate aggregate demand in the economy.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act 2003 provides for the responsibility of the Central Government to ensure inter-generational equity in fiscal management. Statement II: As per the Ricardian Equivalence, the government borrowings today will come at lesser cost than future borrowings to stimulate aggregate demand in the economy. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**

Statement I is correct:**The Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act, 2003, is an Act to provide for the **responsibility of the Central Government to ensure inter-generational equity in fiscal management and long-term **macro-economic stability by removing fiscal impediment**s in the effective conduct of monetary policy and prudential debt management consistent with fiscal sustainability through limits on the Central Government borrowings, debt and deficits, greater transparency in fiscal operations of the Central Government and conducting fiscal policy in a medium-term frameworkIt set targets for the phased reduction of the fiscal deficit to 3% of the GDP and the debt of the central government to 40% of GDP thereby reducing debt to a sustainable level over a medium term so as to ensure inter-generational equity in fiscal management and long term macro-economic stability.**

Statement II is incorrect: **Ricardian Equivalence claims that when governments finance expenditures through debt rather than immediate taxation, rational consumers anticipate future tax increases. Consequently, they increase their savings to offset the future tax burden, rendering fiscal stimulus ineffective. As per Ricardian Equivalence method by which a government finances its spending—whether through taxes today or debt that necessitates higher taxes tomorrow—has no net effect on overall demand within the economy.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to Indian economy, consider the following goods:
I. Street lighting
II. Rice given in Public Distribution System
III. Policing/Police Services
IV. Medicines sold at a chemist shop
V. Flood Control
How many of the above can be considered as the Public good in India?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian economy, consider the following goods: I. Street lighting II. Rice given in Public Distribution System III. Policing/Police Services IV. Medicines sold at a chemist shop V. Flood Control How many of the above can be considered as the Public good in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Options I, III and V are correct: **Goods can be classified on the basis of ownership into public goods and private goods. Public goods are owned and enjoyed collectively by society and are available to all people without discrimination. They possess two essential characteristics: non-rivalry, meaning that consumption by one person does not reduce the availability of the good for others, and non-excludability, meaning that no one can be prevented from accessing or enjoying the good**. **The examples for public goods that exhibit these characteristics include: **Defence, Police**, Street-light, Flood **Control,**roads, bridges, park, town hall etc. Option II and IV are incorrect: Rice distributed through the Public Distribution System (PDS)**is**excludable**, as only eligible beneficiaries with ration cards can access it and cannot be considered as the public good. Further Medicine is not considered as the public good as it does not fulfil both the characteristics.**Medicines are not public goods **because they are both rivalrous as consumption by one reduces availability for others and excludable as can be restricted by pricing and access.

statementLines: Consider the following:
Demographic performance 2.
Forest and ecology 3.
Governance reforms 4.

Tax and fiscal efforts For the horizontal tax devolution, the Fifteenth Finance Commission used

questions.questionText: Consider the following: 1. Demographic performance 2. Forest and ecology 3. Governance reforms 4. Stable government 5. Tax and fiscal efforts For the horizontal tax devolution, the Fifteenth Finance Commission used how many of the above as criteria other than population, area and income distance?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.** Option 1 is correct
- **Demographic performance refers to the assessment of population-related factors such as population growth rate, demographic composition, and demographic changes over time. The Commission used 2011 population data for its recommendations. States with a lower fertility ratio will be scored higher on this criterion. The 15th Finance Commission suggested 12.5% weightage to the Demographic performance.** Option 2 is correct
- **Forest and ecology refer to the assessment of environmental factors, including the preservation and conservation of forests, biodiversity, and ecological balance. This criterion has been determined by calculating the proportion of dense forest within each state relative to the total dense forest across all states. The 15th Finance Commission suggested 10% weightage to the Forest and Ecology.** Option 3 is incorrect
- **Governance reforms encompass various measures aimed at improving the efficiency, transparency, and effectiveness of government operations and public service delivery. This criterion was not considered by the Fifteenth Finance Commission as one of the criteria for horizontal tax devolution.** Option 4 is incorrect
- **Stable government refers to the political stability and continuity of government institutions. While stability in government can be a desirable factor, it was not explicitly mentioned as a criterion for horizontal tax devolution by the Fifteenth Finance Commission.** Option 5 is correct
- **Tax and fiscal efforts involve the assessment of a state’s efforts in tax collection and fiscal management. This criterion has been employed to incentivize states with better efficiency in tax collection. It is determined by the ratio of the average per capita own tax revenue to the average per capita state GDP over a three-year period from**2016-17**to**2018-19**. The 15th Finance Commission suggested 2.5% weightage to the Tax and fiscal efforts.

III. Stabilizing the level of aggregate demand in economy

questions.questionText: Which of the following are considered as the objectives of the fiscal policy of Government of India? I. Ensuring distribution of free goods II. Redistribution of income and reducing economic inequality III. Stabilizing the level of aggregate demand in economy Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The government plays a crucial role in enhancing the welfare of the people. To achieve this, it formulates a fiscal policy every year with specific objectives** Option I is incorrect: Allocation of public goods (not free goods) to all the people is one of the key objectives of **Fiscal policy of Government of India by providing certain goods and services which cannot be provided by the **market mechanism in the Indian economy**. Examples of such goods are national defence, roads, government administration etc. Free goods which are different from public goods, it refers to the goods that are available in abundance without any cost or human effort. Example: Sunshine  Option II is correct: Redistribution of income and reducing economic inequality is one of the key objectives of the **fiscal policy of the Government of India**. Under this function, the government collects taxes from firms and households and redistributes the revenue to poorer sections of society in the form of government transfers, welfare programmes, and social security schemes.**Taxation and income transfers to the poorest segment of society**are the most direct way to keep inequality in check and reduce poverty in the short term.** Option III is correct:**The overall level of employment and prices in an economy depends on the level of aggregate demand, which in turn is influenced by the spending decisions of millions of private economic agents along with government expenditure.**As part of fiscal policy, the government intervenes to stabilise aggregate demand in order **to correct fluctuations in income, output and employment. For eg., recent rationalisation of GST rates. **

statementLines: With reference to Union Budget, consider the following statements about Grant in aids:
Statement I: Grants-in-aid from foreign countries and international organisations are considered as revenue receipts of Government of India.
Statement II: Grants-in-aid do not create any repayment liability or future claim on the Government of India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to Union Budget, consider the following statements about Grant in aids: Statement I: Grants-in-aid from foreign countries and international organisations are considered as revenue receipts of Government of India. Statement II: Grants-in-aid do not create any repayment liability or future claim on the Government of India. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **A budget is a financial statement that contains the government’s receipts and expenditure for a particular financial year. The Union Budget is divided into two major parts: the Revenue Account and Capital Account with various items listed under each based on their nature.**

Statement I is correct:**Revenue receipts are those receipts that do not lead to a claim on the government and are non-redeemable. They are divided into tax and non-tax revenues.**Grants-in-aid from foreign countries and **international organisations is classified as the non-tax revenue under Revenue receipts of the union budget. **

Statement II is correct:**Unlike loans, grants-in-aid**received from foreign countries and international organisations**do not create any repayment liability for the government and do not give rise to any future claim on government **Grants-in-aid **from foreign countries and international organisations**are considered as the revenue receipts under **the Union Budget as **it does not**create any repayment liability and claim on the government. Hence option a is **correct: **Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I

questions.questionText: Consider the following major reforms/events in the evolution of Independent India’s Budgetary process and arrange them in the correct chronological order: I. questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Option I: Gender Budgeting **was formally introduced in the**Union Budget 2005–06**when the Ministry of Finance included the Gender Budget Statement for the first time. Its objective was to assess how budgetary allocations impact women and promote gender-responsive planning.**Option III: **In Union Budget 2017-18, the Government of India advanced the Union Budget date from the last working day of February to 1st February,**and in the same year the Rail Budget was also merged with the General Budget.**Option II: **The government's annual financial statement was presented in digital format for the first time on February 1, 2021, due to the Covid-19 pandemic. questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: If over successive years the difference between the Fiscal Deficit and the Primary Deficit keeps widening, it most appropriately reflects:
questions.questionText: If over successive years the difference between the Fiscal Deficit and the Primary Deficit keeps widening, it most appropriately reflects:

options.a: A decline in the government’s overall interest payment obligations
options.b: A rising burden of government’s interest payments on past debt
options.c: A contraction in government’s revenue expenditure relative to capital expenditure
options.d: An increase in the government’s non-debt capital receipts over the years

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Fiscal Deficit**refers to the total borrowing requirement of the government in a financial year. It includes all government expenditures, including the interest payments on past debt. It is the**difference between the government’s total expenditure and its total receipts excluding borrowing **-**Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure – Total Non-Debt Receipts **Primary Deficit **is the**difference between the current year’s fiscal deficit and the interest paid on the borrowings **of the previous years.**It reflects the fiscal stance of the government without the burden of past loans.**Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit – Interest Payments **Thus,** Interest payments = Fiscal Deficit – Primary Deficit**.**What does a widening gap indicate? **If over successive years the**difference between the fiscal deficit and the primary deficit keeps increasing**, it means: -**Interest payments are rising faster than other components of the deficit.**- A larger portion of the government’s fiscal deficit is now being used**just to service past loans**, not for new development spending. - This signals a**growing debt burden**and increasing pressure on the government’s finances.**Numerical Example: **Year **Fiscal Deficit (₹**lakh crore) Primary Deficit (₹ lakh **crore) **Gap = Interest Payments **2021 10 5 5 2022 10.5 4.5 6 2023 11 4 7 In 2021, interest payments = 10 – 5 = ₹5 lakh crore In 2022, interest payments = 10.5 – 4.5 = ₹6 lakh crore In 2023, interest payments = 11 – 4 = ₹7 lakh crore**Here you can see the difference keeps widening from 5 →**6 →** 7.**This clearly shows: - The government is paying**more and more interest**each year. - The burden of servicing past debt is rising. - Less fiscal space is left for productive expenditure.**Therefore, the widening gap most appropriately reflects:**A rising burden of interest payments on past debt** (Option b).

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?leec1=5-6 Page: 72 Forum IAS Indian Economy Red Book (Second Edition), Chapter: 5 - Public Finance and Fiscal Policy, Page: 60.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**

Statement I is correct:**Capital receipts are those receipts of the government which create liability or reduce financial assets.**

Statement II is correct:**Borrowings—such as loans from the RBI, foreign governments, or through the issuance of government securities—and disinvestment, which entails selling the government’s stake in public sector enterprises, are categorized as capital receipts.**

Statement III is incorrect:** Interest received on loans given by the Central government to the states or other countries is classified as a revenue receipt since it represents income for the government and does not result in any liability.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: If the Government of India chooses to implement a counter-cyclical fiscal policy when the Indian economy is undergoing a recession, which of the following measures is it most likely to undertake?
I. Increased spending on social programmes
II. Reduction of income tax
III. Increasing the investment in infrastructure sector
IV. Selling of government securities in market
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: If the Government of India chooses to implement a counter-cyclical fiscal policy when the Indian economy is undergoing a recession, which of the following measures is it most likely to undertake? I. Increased spending on social programmes II. Reduction of income tax III. Increasing the investment in infrastructure sector IV. Selling of government securities in market Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **If the government follows an anti-cyclical (i.e., countercyclical) fiscal stance during the recession in the Indian economy, the government increases its expenditure and reduces the tax burden of the people to put more money in hands of people and boosting demand in the economy.** Option I is correct:**Increased government spending on social programmes is part of a**counter-cyclical fiscal policy**during periods of slowdown or recession as it raises household income, thereby stimulating aggregate demand, which in turn contributes to higher economic growth.** Option II is correct:**A**reduction in income tax is a feature of a**counter-cyclical fiscal policy**during periods of recession as it increases the disposable income of individuals, thereby boosting consumption demand and supporting higher economic growth** Option III is correct:**An increase in **the investment in the infrastructure sector**is considered a counter-cyclical**fiscal policy**measure during a recession as it leads to increase in the overall government spending and therefore supports aggregate demand and economic growth.** Option IV is incorrect:**Selling government securities is not** a counter-cyclical fiscal policy**during a recession as it withdraws liquidity from the economy, resulting in a contractionary impact probably and deepening the recession situation in the economy. Knowledge Base: - The fiscal stance refers to the overall orientation or approach of a government’s fiscal policy, particularly reflected through its pattern of public expenditure. - Economics broadly classifies fiscal stance into two types based on how government spending behaves relative to the economic cycle as follows**Anti-cyclical (Countercyclical) Fiscal Stance:**When the government increases spending during periods of economic slowdown, recession, or demand slackness, it is said to follow an anti-cyclical stance. The objective of such a policy is to stimulate the economy and boost aggregate demand during bad times.**Pro-cyclical Fiscal Stance:** When the government increases spending during economic expansion or growth phases, it is following a pro-cyclical stance. The objective of the pro cyclical approach is spending rises in good times rather than in downturns.

statementLines: Consider the following expenditures made by the Government of India:
I. Loan given to state government for constructing a river bridge.
II. Interest payment on loans taken from the World Bank.
III. Acquisition of shares in Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs).
IV. Repayment of the principal amount of past loans taken from foreign institutions.
Expenditure used for
how many of the above purposes is classified as 'Capital Expenditure' in the Union Budget?
questions.questionText: Consider the following expenditures made by the Government of India: I. Loan given to state government for constructing a river bridge. II. Interest payment on loans taken from the World Bank. III. Acquisition of shares in Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs). IV. Repayment of the principal amount of past loans taken from foreign institutions. Expenditure used for how many of the above purposes is classified as 'Capital Expenditure' in the Union Budget?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**Capital expenditures are expenditures of the government which result**in creation of physical or financial assets or **reduction in financial liabilities**. In contrast, revenue expenditure are those expenditures which do not create any assets. These are the expenditures incurred for the routine, usual and normal day to day running of the government.** Option I is correct.**When the Central Government gives a loan to a state government, it creates a**financial asset**for the Central Government. Therefore, it is considered as a**capital expenditure. ** Option II is incorrect.**Interest payment is a recurring expense. It does**not reduce the principal loan nor create any **asset**. Therefore, interest payments are considered as**revenue expenditure**.** Option III is correct.**When the government buys**shares in Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs**) it acquires a**financial **asset**. Therefore it falls under the capital** expenditure**of the budget.** Option IV is correct.**Repaying the principal amount of a loan results in the**reduction in a financial liability**for the government. Therefore it is classified as**capital expenditure** in the budget.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to Indian economy, consider the following tools of fiscal policy:
I. Proportional taxation
II. Fiscal stimulus packages
III. Food subsidies
How many of the above function as automatic stabilisers in an economy?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian economy, consider the following tools of fiscal policy: I. Proportional taxation II. Fiscal stimulus packages III. Food subsidies How many of the above function as automatic stabilisers in an economy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**Automatic stabilisers are fiscal tools that work**on their own**, without any deliberate government action, to reduce fluctuations in GDP. They help stabilise the economy by increasing spending during recessions and reducing excess demand during booms.**

Statement I is correct.**Under**proportional taxation**, the government collects a fixed fraction of people’s income as tax. Proportional taxes help**stabilise the economy by making disposable income**and consumer spending**less **affected by changes in GDP**. When GDP rises, people earn more, but part of that extra income automatically goes as taxes, so consumption doesn’t increase too sharply. During a recession, when incomes fall, tax payments also fall automatically, so people still retain some income. This prevents consumption from dropping too much and helps reduce the overall fall in demand, keeping the economy more stable. Thus proportional taxation is an example of**automatic stabiliser**.**

Statement II is incorrect.**A**fiscal stimulus package**usually means a**deliberate increase in government spending**by the government to fight a slowdown or recession. This requires active policy decisions by the government and does not switch on and off automatically with changes in GDP.**

Statement III is correct.**Food subsidies**such as food security programs increase support when incomes fall. During recessions more people qualify for subsidised food, which helps stabilise consumption and prevents a sharp decline in aggregate demand. During boom fewer people rely on subsidies, reducing government spending automatically. Since this adjustment happens without any government intervention, food subsidies act as**automatic stabilisers**.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**

Option a is incorrect.**When total**revenue exceeds total expenditure,**it is known as**budget surplus.**The golden rule does**not talk about the budget to be always in surplus**, rather it specifies how borrowed money should be used.**

Option b is incorrect. **The golden rule**does not specify a limit on fiscal deficit**. Instead, it focuses on the quality of expenditure. The**3% fiscal deficit limit**comes from the**FRBM Act**(Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act), 2003, and is not related to the golden rule.**

Option c is incorrect.**Revenue deficit**arises when revenue expenditure (salaries, subsidies, interest payments, etc.) exceeds revenue receipts. Using borrowed money for such expenses is considered**fiscally unhealthy**because it does not create any future returns. The**Golden Rule**explicitly**discourages **this kind of expenditure from borrowed money**.**

Option d is correct.**The**golden rule**stipulates that over the economic cycle, the government should**borrow only to **invest**and**not to fund current spending**. In essence,**whatever the government borrows, it must use it for **productive investments like infrastructure, hospitals, or R&D**. These investments are productive assets that generate future income or benefits. More specifically, the golden rule requires the**budget to be in balance or surplus** over the economic cycle.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to fiscal policy, which of the following are the potential consequences of a persistent and high fiscal deficit in an economy?
I. Increase in the debt-to-GDP ratio.
II. Crowding out of private sector investments.
III. High Cost-push Inflation.
IV. Decrease in the government's debt-servicing costs.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to fiscal policy, which of the following are the potential consequences of a persistent and high fiscal deficit in an economy? I. Increase in the debt-to-GDP ratio. II. Crowding out of private sector investments. III. High Cost-push Inflation. IV. Decrease in the government's debt-servicing costs. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Fiscal Deficit**is the difference between the government’s total expenditure and its total receipts excluding borrowing. The fiscal deficit is financed through borrowing and hence, it indicates the total borrowing requirements of the government from all sources. Gross fiscal deficit = Total expenditure – (Revenue receipts + non-debt creating capital receipts)** Option I is correct.**A**persistent fiscal deficit**means the government has to**borrow heavily**every year to bridge the gap. This continuous borrowing directly**adds to the national debt**. If the**growth rate of this debt exceeds**the**growth **rate of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP**) the**debt-to-GDP ratio will increase**. Therefore, persistent increase in the fiscal deficit of a country may result in increasing**debt-to-GDP ratio.** Option II is correct.**When the**government borrows heavily**from banks and financial markets to fund a**persistent **fiscal deficit**it**competes with private companies**for the same pool of domestic savings. This**higher demand for **funds**can raise interest rates and make**credit more expensive or less available**for businesses. As a result private firms may cut back or postpone investment because borrowing costs are too high, which is known as**crowding out of **private investment. **Thus,**persistent fiscal deficit**may result in**crowding out**of private investment.** Option III is correct.**When the government becomes a major borrower in the financial market, the high** demand for **funds can push interest rates up. **Higher interest rates**increase the cost of capital for businesses. **The businesses facing higher production costs often pass those costs onto consumers by** raising the prices of goods and services. **This type of price rise driven by higher costs is known as cost-push inflation. Thus, a**high and persistent fiscal deficit**can contribute to**cost-push inflation.** Option IV is incorrect.**As the**national debt rises**due to**persistent deficits**, the government's**obligation to pay **interest **on that debt also increases. These interest payments, known as**debt servicing costs, increase** and can become difficult to manage. The debt servicing costs do not create any new assets or infrastructure. As this burden grows a larger part of the government’s income gets used just to pay interest, leaving less money for essential services like health, education, welfare schemes and infrastructure development.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Suppose the revenue expenditure is ₹ 1,10,000 crores and the revenue receipts of the Government are ₹ 90,000 crores. The Government budget also shows borrowings of ₹ 60,000 crores and interest payments of ₹ 50,000 crores.
Which of the following statements are correct?
I. Revenue deficit is ₹ 20,000 crores.
II. Fiscal deficit is ₹ 60,000 crores.
III. Primary deficit is ₹ 10,000 crores.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Suppose the revenue expenditure is ₹ 1,10,000 crores and the revenue receipts of the Government are ₹ 90,000 crores. The Government budget also shows borrowings of ₹ 60,000 crores and interest payments of ₹ 50,000 crores. Which of the following statements are correct? I. Revenue deficit is ₹ 20,000 crores. II. Fiscal deficit is ₹ 60,000 crores. III. Primary deficit is ₹ 10,000 crores. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**.**Given Data: **Revenue Expenditure = ₹1,10,000 crores Revenue Receipts = ₹90,000 crores Government Borrowings (Fiscal Deficit) = ₹60,000 crores Interest Payments = ₹50,000 crores**

Statement I is correct**.**Revenue Deficit**shows the shortfall of revenue receipts compared to revenue expenditure. It means the government is not even able to cover its regular, day-to-day expenses with its revenue income.**Formula: Revenue Deficit = Revenue Expenditure – Revenue Receipts **Calculation:**= ₹1,10,000 crore – ₹90,000 crore =**₹20,000 crore**The government is earning ₹90,000 crore through taxes and other revenue sources, but spending ₹1,10,000 crore on daily operations. So, it is falling short by ₹20,000 crore. This gap is the**revenue deficit**.**

Statement II is correct**.**Fiscal Deficit**is the total borrowing requirement of the government. It shows the gap between what the government plans to spend and what it earns through non-borrowed sources like taxes and other income. Formula: Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure – Total Receipts (excluding borrowings) **Calculation:**Borrowings are given directly as**₹60,000 crore.**In this case, the government is borrowing ₹60,000 crore to bridge this gap. That means, after using up all its income (except borrowings), the remaining shortfall of ₹60,000 crore is being met entirely through borrowings. Therefore, the**fiscal deficit in this scenario is ₹**60,000 crore** which is exactly equal to the amount borrowed.
**Statement III is correct**. **Primary Deficit** shows the fiscal deficit after removing interest payments. It helps understand the borrowing done for purposes other than interest payments.
**Formula: Primary Deficit = Fiscal Deficit – Interest Payments Calculation:** = ₹60,000 crore – ₹50,000 crore = **₹10,000 crore** Out of the ₹60,000 crore borrowed, ₹50,000 crore is used just to pay interest on previous loans. That means only ₹10,000 crore is being borrowed to fund current year’s non-interest spending — this is the **primary deficit**.**

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which one of the following is likely to be the most inflationary in its effects?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is likely to be the most inflationary in its effects?

options.b: Borrowing from the public to finance budget deficit
options.c: Borrowing from the banks to finance a budget deficit
options.d: Creation of new money to finance a budget deficit

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. Deficit financing means generating funds to finance the deficit which results from excess expenditure over revenue.
The gap is being covered by borrowing from the public by the sale of bonds or by printing new money. Government expenditure by printing money boosts incomes and raises private demand in the economy. Thus, it fuels inflation. A little increase in inflation is healthy as it encourages business activity. But if the government doesn’t stop in time, more and more money floods the market and creates high inflation. And since inflation is revealed with a lag, it is often too late before governments realise they have over-borrowed. Higher inflation and higher government debt provide grounds for macroeconomic instability.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: This type of budgeting involves a method of budget preparation in which no expenditure is assumed to be pre- sanctioned. Instead, each department must justify every amount it requests. It is intended to eliminate outdated schemes and promote the efficient allocation of resources.
Which one of the following types of budget is being described above?
questions.questionText: This type of budgeting involves a method of budget preparation in which no expenditure is assumed to be pre- sanctioned. Instead, each department must justify every amount it requests. It is intended to eliminate outdated schemes and promote the efficient allocation of resources. Which one of the following types of budget is being described above?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**

Option a is incorrect**.**Outcome Budget **focuses on the**results**or**outcomes**achieved from the money spent. Under it, the emphasis is on whether the allocated funds are delivering the intended**social or economic impact**. For example, if money was spent on a rural education programme, the outcome budget would track whether enrolment and learning levels actually improved. It is a**forward-looking tool**that links**financial outlays with measurable outcomes**.**

Option b is incorrect**.**The Performance Budget **measures the**efficiency**of public spending. It focuses on comparing the**targets set for a department or scheme**with the**actual physical achievements**. For instance, if a scheme aimed to build 100 km of rural roads but only built 60 km, the performance budget highlights this gap. It is useful for improving government accountability and making budgeting more result-oriented.**

Option c is correct**.**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)**is a system where every department must**justify its entire budget **from scratch**, without assuming that last year’s expenditure is the base. The budgeting process starts from**zero**, and every activity is evaluated based on its**current relevance, cost-effectiveness**, and alignment with government priorities. Unnecessary or outdated schemes can be**discontinued**. New schemes must show why they deserve funds. This method improves**transparency**, eliminates inefficiency, and helps the government**spend smarter**, especially in times of fiscal constraint.**

Option d is incorrect**.**Gender Budgeting **approach aims to ensure**gender equity**in the allocation of public resources. It does not refer to a separate budget, but rather to**mainstreaming gender concerns** in the general budgeting process. Schemes are evaluated based on how they impact women or other marginalised genders.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Fiscal drag may reduce the overall demand in an economy.
Statement II: A progressive tax system may result in higher tax liability as income of individuals increases.
Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: Fiscal drag may reduce the overall demand in an economy. Statement II: A progressive tax system may result in higher tax liability as income of individuals increases. Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct.
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. In any economy, the way taxation is structured can affect people's ability to spend. A**progressive tax system**, where tax rates increase with income, is designed to ensure fairness. However, if tax slabs are not updated regularly in line with inflation or income growth, a situation called**fiscal drag**can occur. This can**reduce the real purchasing power**of individuals over time and affect demand in the broader economy.**

Statement I is correct**.**Fiscal drag**happens when rising incomes push individuals into**higher tax brackets**, but the tax structure does not adjust in time. As a result, people pay**a larger portion of their income as tax**, and their**disposable income increases more slowly**than their earnings. And when people have**less money to spend**, overall**household consumption falls**. Since consumption is a major part of aggregate demand, fiscal drag may**lead to a decline in demand**in the economy.**

Statement II is correct**. In a progressive tax system, tax rates rise as income increases. As a result, inflation-induced increases in nominal income can push individuals into higher tax brackets and increase their tax liability. So, as income increases,**a larger part of it goes to taxes**, which results in a higher**tax burden**for individuals.**Statement II explains Statement I**. Fiscal drag is a**direct outcome of the progressive tax system. **When people move into higher tax slabs without any real increase in their standard of living (because tax slabs are not adjusted for inflation), their**disposable income remains less**. This affects their ability to spend more, which in turn**reduces **demand** in the economy.

statementLines: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following statements:
1. A share of the household financial savings goes towards government borrowings.
2. Dated securities issued at market-related rates in auctions form a large component of internal debt.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following statements: 1. A share of the household financial savings goes towards government borrowings. 2. Dated securities issued at market-related rates in auctions form a large component of internal debt. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation: Option c is the correct answer.**

Statement 1 is correct:**Gross financial savings of households include money saved in banks, provident funds, pension schemes, shares, insurance and in the form of currency, among other categories.**These savings form the pool of **domestic finance for investments **which are crucial to job creation and are currently critical as they will be crucial in deciding the pace of economic revival. Deposits with banks are the single largest form of households’ financial assets, followed by insurance funds, mutual funds and currency. Therefore,**any adverse movement in the household savings will have a significant bearing on **banks, insurance companies and mutual/provident funds,**who, in turn, are key investors in government **securities.**

Statement 2 is correct:**Internal debt constitutes over 94 per cent of the overall public debt. Internal loans that make up for the bulk of public debt are further divided into two broad categories – marketable and non-marketable debt.**The sources of marketable public debt are dated government securities (G-Secs),** treasury bills, external assistance, and short-term borrowings.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to the Union Budget 2025-26, consider the following statements:
I. India’s fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP has steadily declined since Financial Year 2021–22.
II. Short term borrowing as a source of deficit financing has steadily declined over the past five years.
Which of the statements given above is/are incorrect?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Union Budget 2025-26, consider the following statements: I. India’s fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP has steadily declined since Financial Year 2021–22. II. Short term borrowing as a source of deficit financing has steadily declined over the past five years. Which of the statements given above is/are incorrect?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**A**fiscal deficit**occurs when the government’s total expenditure exceeds its total revenue (excluding borrowings). To bridge this gap, the government relies on various sources of financing such as market borrowings, short-term borrowings, and funds from the**National Small Savings Fund (NSSF)**, etc.**

Statement I is correct.**The fiscal deficit peaked in**2020–21**at 9.2% of GDP due to COVID-19. After FY 2021–22, the fiscal deficit has**consistently fallen year after year**moving from above 6.7% (**2021-22**) to 6.4% (**2022-23**) to 5.6% and 4.8% in**2023-24**and**2024-25**respectively. The**Budget Estimate (BE) target of 2025–26 is 4.4% **which is lower than the previous year target of 4.8%. This reflects the government’s ongoing fiscal consolidation strategy.**

Statement II is incorrect.**Short term borrowings (Treasury Bills, etc.) are**funds borrowed for less than one year**to meet immediate financing needs. From the above figure, the short-term borrowings**do not**show a**steady decline**over the five years starting from**2021-22**. Short term borrowing as percentage of Fiscal deficit was 0.8 % in**2021-22**which increased to 1.1% in**2022-23**, and again declined to 0.5% in**2023-24**. It became-1.2% in**2024-25**and 0 in budget estimates**2025-26**.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to financing models, which of the following best describes the concept of ‘Mezzanine Financing’?
questions.questionText: With reference to financing models, which of the following best describes the concept of ‘Mezzanine Financing’?

options.a: A hybrid source of capital that is partly debt and partly equity.
options.b: A short-term stopgap loan taken until long-term funds are secured.
options.c: Money invested in a business in return for ownership share.
options.d: A type of no collateral loan given mainly to high growth firms.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Option a is correct: Mezzanine financing is a hybrid source of capital that combines features of both debt and equity, positioned between senior loans and equity in repayment priority. It usually carries higher returns and may include equity-linked features such as warrants or conversion rights. Firms use it when they need funds beyond traditional debt capacity but want to avoid equity dilution. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy is exploring**innovative financing models **including**Contracts for **Difference (CfDs)**and**mezzanine financing **to help mobilize**up to USD 1 trillion**of the investment required for India’s**renewable-energy expansion**. India needs around**USD 1.5 trillion by 2030**to reach its**500 GW non-fossil **capacity target**, and mezzanine financing is being considered as a tool to**reduce investment risk**,**attract private **capital**, and**strengthen green financing mechanisms**.
Option b is incorrect: A short-term stopgap loan taken until long-term funding is arranged, commonly known as bridge financing. Such loans are temporary, have no equity component, and do not occupy a subordinated position in the capital structure.
Option c is incorrect: Pure equity financing is where investors receive ownership of shares and voting rights in return for capital. Equity holders are residual claimants and have no fixed repayment schedule.
Option d is incorrect: Loans extended to high-growth firms without substantial collateral, similar to venture debt. Such loans rely on a company’s future potential, but they do not have subordinated ranking, nor do they include equity-linked incentives..

○ This reflects fiscal federalism and supports State-level development and welfare spending.
- **Central Sector Schemes (16%)** (excluding capital outlay on defence and subsidies) ○ These are schemes **fully funded and implemented by the Union Government**, such as national flagship programmes.
**Centrally Sponsored Schemes**are programmes where funding is shared between Centre and States, such as PM Awas Yojana, PM Poshan, and Jal Jeevan Mission. This amounts to around** 8% **of total expenditure. -**Finance Commission and Other Transfers (8%)**○ Represents grants and transfers to States beyond tax devolution, including revenue deficit and sector-specific grants. -**Other Expenditure (8%)** ○ Includes miscellaneous administrative and operational expenses not classified elsewhere.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Recently, the Government of India launched the ‘Gyan Bharatam Mission’ under the Digital India programme. In this context, what is its primary objective?
questions.questionText: Recently, the Government of India launched the ‘Gyan Bharatam Mission’ under the Digital India programme. In this context, what is its primary objective?

options.a: Improve foundational literacy and numeracy among children
options.b: Provide digital literacy training to rural artisans
options.c: Survey, conserve, and digitise India’s manuscript heritage
options.d: To promote scientific research in the field of traditional medicine

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. India has a vast and rich manuscript heritage, covering subjects such as religion, science, mathematics, medicine, and the arts. Most of these manuscripts are scattered across the country and are not accessible for public knowledge or research. The Gyan Bharatam Mission aims to bridge this gap. Launched under the**Digital India**initiative in the Union Budget 2025–26, the**Gyan Bharatam Mission is focused on**:**1. Surveying**manuscript collections across India**2. Documenting and conserving**them**3. Digitising**them into a National Digital Repository 4. Making them**accessible**for research, academic use, and public dissemination This is an effort to**unlock centuries of traditional Indian knowledge and make it available in digital format for **educational and cultural purposes**.**

Option a is incorrect**. Improving foundational literacy and numeracy is the focus of the**NIPUN Bharat Mission**under the**Ministry of Education**. Its goal is to ensure that every child in India attains the desired learning competencies in reading, writing, and arithmetic by the end of Grade 3. It is crucial for human capital development in India.**

Option b is incorrect**. The focus on empowering rural artisans with digital tools is being taken up under schemes like the**PM Vishwakarma Yojana**. The nodal** ministry**for this scheme is the**Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium **Enterprises (MoMSME)**. The scheme is implemented with the support of the**Ministry of Skill Development & **Entrepreneurship (MSDE)**for training components, and the**Department of Financial Services (DFS)**under the**Ministry of Finance**for facilitating access to**collateral-free credit. **

Option d is incorrect**. Promotion of research in traditional medicine is primarily the domain of the**Ministry of **AYUSH**. Several schemes under the ministry such as the**AYUSH Grid**, and**Ayurveda Gyan Naipunya Initiative (AGNI)** etc. are designed to encourage scientific research, clinical trials, and innovation in systems like Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following best describes the term ‘Twin Deficit Problem’?
The simultaneous occurrence of:
questions.questionText: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following best describes the term ‘Twin Deficit Problem’? The simultaneous occurrence of:

options.a: High public debt and High inflation
options.c: Unemployment and fall in public investment
options.d: Current account deficit and fiscal deficit.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. The**Twin Deficit Problem**is a well-known macroeconomic challenge that emerges when a country experiences both a**fiscal deficit**and a**current account deficit**at the same time. - A**fiscal deficit**means the government’s expenditure exceeds its non-borrowed receipts. - A**current account deficit**occurs when the value of imports of goods, services, and transfers surpasses the value of exports. When both deficits run high together, it signals structural imbalances in the economy. It can lead to depreciation of the currency, loss of investor confidence, and higher borrowing costs all of which make macroeconomic management more difficult. Knowledge Base:**Twin Deficit Crisis of 1991 **A higher twin deficit is inherently destabilizing and was the primary reason why India faced a currency crisis back in 1991. The**fiscal deficit**had reached an alarming level of**8.4% of GDP**in 1990–91. Within this, the**revenue deficit was 3.4%**, indicating that the government was borrowing even to meet routine expenditures. A large part of this deficit was financed through borrowing from the Reserve Bank of India, leading to excess money supply and rising inflation. Simultaneously, the**current account deficit (CAD)**had widened to**3.1% of GDP**. This was driven by a surge in imports, particularly crude oil due to the Gulf War, while exports remained stagnant. The situation worsened as**India’s foreign **exchange reserves fell to just $1.2 billion**, enough to finance less than**three weeks of imports**. The crisis reached its peak when India was forced to**pledge 67 tonnes of gold**to secure emergency loans. It was on the verge of default, and the**IMF stepped in**, demanding structural reforms in exchange for assistance. The link between the two deficits was clear. The large fiscal deficit stimulated domestic demand, part of which spilled over into higher imports, thereby widening the current account deficit. This created a**vicious cycle**, worsening both deficits and shaking investor confidence. The crisis ultimately paved the way for the**1991 economic reforms**, led by**Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao**and**Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh**, marking the beginning of India’s liberalisation and economic restructuring.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following statements regarding the Ways and Means
Advances (WMA):
I. The WMA window is intended only to address the temporary mismatches in cash flow of receipts and payments of governments.
II. The interest rate charged on advances under WMA is linked to the reverse repo rate.
Which of the above given statements is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following statements regarding the Ways and Means Advances (WMA): I. The WMA window is intended only to address the temporary mismatches in cash flow of receipts and payments of governments. II. The interest rate charged on advances under WMA is linked to the reverse repo rate. Which of the above given statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The**Ways and Means Advances (WMA)**is a mechanism introduced by the**Reserve Bank of India (RBI)**to provide temporary financial support to the**Central and State Governments**. It helps them meet**short-term mismatches in **cash flow**, especially during periods when revenue collections fall short of immediate expenditure needs. This system was formally institutionalised following an**agreement signed between the RBI and the Central **Government in March 1997**. Under this agreement, the**earlier practice of issuing ad hoc Treasury Bills to **automatically replenish the Centre’s cash balances was discontinued**from**April 1, 1997**. This move was part of a broader push towards fiscal discipline and transparency in public finance**

Statement I is correct**. The WMA facility is specifically designed to**bridge temporary gaps**between government receipts and payments. It allows governments to**tide over mismatches in their day-to-day cash flows**. The usage of this window is strictly time-bound and limited (not later than three months from the date of the making of the advance), ensuring that it is not misused as a long-term borrowing route.**

Statement II is incorrect**. The interest rate on WMA is the prevailing repo rate and on overdraft it is 2 per cent above the repo rate. As per RBI norms, overdrafts attract a penal interest rate, which is typically 2 percent higher than the repo rate if availed up to 100 per cent of WMA limit and 5 percent higher than repo rate if it exceeds 100 per cent of WMA limit.**The interest rate charged on advances under WMA is linked to the repo rate and not the reverse repo **rate. **Knowledge Base:**Normal Ways and Means Advances:**Normal WMA**is the**basic form of temporary advance**provided by the RBI to the government — both Central and State. It is an**unsecured advance**, meaning**no collateral is required**for borrowing under this category. The**limits for **Normal WMA**are**fixed in consultation**between the RBI and the Government and are reviewed periodically. Interest on Normal WMA is generally charged at the**repo rate**. The loan must be**repaid within the stipulated period**(typically up to 90 days), failing which it may be treated as an overdraft with higher interest. Special Ways and Means Advances (Special Drawing Facility — SDF):**Special WMA**, also known as**Special Drawing Facility (SDF)**, is an additional type of advance available**only to State **Governments/UTs**. It is a**secured advance**, provided**against the pledge of government securities**held by the State, such as central government dated securities or other approved securities. Because of the security backing, the**interest rate on Special WMA is lower**— typically**one percentage point below the repo rate**. States are generally allowed to**avail Special WMA first**. Once the Special WMA limit is exhausted, they can draw on Normal WMA up to their respective limit.**Feature **Normal WMA **Special WMA (SDF) ** Collateral Needed No Yes, backed by approved securities Interest Rate At repo rate Repo rate minus one percentage point Priority of Access After Special WMA for States/UTs First for States/UTs if securities are available Purpose General short‑term cash flow support Short‑term support with cheaper cost

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to an economy, consider the following statements:
Statement-I: In general, the fiscal deficit of the government increases during a recession and decreases during an economic boom period.
Statement-II: During a recession, in general tax revenues of the government fall, while in an economic boom period, they tend to rise.
Statement-III: Governments often follow expansionary fiscal policy during a recession and contractionary fiscal policy during an economic boom period.
Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to an economy, consider the following statements: Statement-I: In general, the fiscal deficit of the government increases during a recession and decreases during an economic boom period. Statement-II: During a recession, in general tax revenues of the government fall, while in an economic boom period, they tend to rise. Statement-III: Governments often follow expansionary fiscal policy during a recession and contractionary fiscal policy during an economic boom period. Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct, but only one of them explains Statement I.
options.c: Only one of the Statement II and III is correct and that explains Statement I.
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**.**Statement I**is**correct**. During a**recession**, Government revenues fall while spending obligations often increase, leading to a**wider fiscal deficit**. Conversely, in an economic**boom period**, revenues are higher and the need for deficit-financed expenditure falls, leading to a**narrower fiscal deficit**.**Statement II**is**correct**.**Tax revenues are closely tied to economic performance**. In a recession, both income and consumption decline, leading to lower tax collections. In contrast, during an economic boom or expansion, tax collections rise automatically with higher economic activity.**Statement III is correc**t. Governments often adopt e**xpansionary fiscal policy during a recession**— increasing spending and cutting taxes to boost demand. Conversely,**during a boom, they may implement contractionary fiscal **policy**to cool down the economy and inflation pressures on the economy.**Statement II and III explains statement I**. The fiscal deficit increases during a recession because tax revenues decline with lower income, profits, and consumption, while government spending often rises to support the economy. During a boom period, tax revenues increase due to higher economic activity and governments usually restrain spending through contractionary fiscal policy. Together, these revenue changes and counter‑cyclical fiscal actions explain why the fiscal deficit widens in a recession and narrows during a boom..

statementLines: What is the difference between “vote-on account” and “interim budget”?
1. The provision of a “vote-on-account’’ is used by a regular Government, while an “interim budget’’ is a provision used by a caretaker Government.
2. A “vote-on-account’’ only deals with the expenditure in the Government budget, while an “interim budget’’ includes both expenditure and receipts.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: What is the difference between “vote-on account” and “interim budget”? 1. The provision of a “vote-on-account’’ is used by a regular Government, while an “interim budget’’ is a provision used by a caretaker Government. 2. A “vote-on-account’’ only deals with the expenditure in the Government budget, while an “interim budget’’ includes both expenditure and receipts. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is incorrect. Vote on account and interim budget can be presented by both the regular government **and the caretaker government. **

Statement 2 is correct. A “vote-on-account’’ only deals with the expenditure in the Government budget, while an **“interim budget’’ includes both expenditure and receipts. **Vote on account is just an interim permission to spend money as against an interim Budget which is an elaborate financial statement of expenditure and receipts including changes in taxes and government policies.

statementLines: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following:
I. Plan borrowings of the Government, including the market borrowings
II. Improve cash forecasting and promote efficient cash management practice for the Government.

questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian economy, consider the following: I. Plan borrowings of the Government, including the market borrowings II. Improve cash forecasting and promote efficient cash management practice for the Government. III. Develop a database system for collecting and maintaining a comprehensive database of Government of India liabilities on a near real time basis How many of the above are the functions of the Public Debt Management Cell (PDMC) to the Government of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Public Debt Management Cell is an interim arrangement before setting up an independent and statutory debt management agency namely the Public Debt Management Agency (PDMA). PDMC has the following advisory functions to the Government."**1. Plan borrowings of the Government, including market borrowings,**other domestic borrowings, SGBs 2. Manage Central Government’s liabilities including NSSF, contingent liabilities. 3. Monitor cash balances of the Government,**improve cash forecasting and promote efficient cash management **practices. **4. Advise other Divisions in DEA on matters related to External Debt involving external borrowings through MI, Bilateral Co-operation, other possible sources, in terms of cost, tenure, currency, hedging requirements, etc., and monitor developments in foreign exchange markets. 5. Foster a liquid and efficient market for Government securities 6. Develop interfaces with various stakeholders/agencies in the regulatory/financial architecture etc. to carry out assigned functions efficiently. 7. Advice on matters related to investment and capital market operations. 8. Undertake research work related to new products development, market development, risk management, debt sustainability assessment, other debt management functions, etc. 9.** Develop a database system for collecting and maintaining a comprehensive database of Government of India **liabilities on a near real time basis** and shall be responsible for publication of relevant information.
10. Carry out Preparatory work for independent PDMA.

statementLines: With reference to economy, consider the following component:
I. House Rent Allowance
II. Unemployment Allowance
III. Old age pension
How many of the above are considered as the transfer payments from the government or firms to the households?
questions.questionText: With reference to economy, consider the following component: I. House Rent Allowance II. Unemployment Allowance III. Old age pension How many of the above are considered as the transfer payments from the government or firms to the households?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Transfer payments refer to payments made by the government or firms to households without receiving any goods or services in return. These payments are meant to ensure social welfare and provide financial support during distress. Since transfer payments do not correspond to any productive activity or production of goods and services, they are excluded from the calculation of national income but are included while calculating the personal income of households.**Options II and III are correct: Unemployment Allowance, gifts, scholarships, pensions (like Old Age Pension or **other Social Security Pensions) are different types of transfer payments made by the government or firms to **households. ** Option I is incorrect: **The House Rent Allowance (HRA) is part of the employees salary and is provided by the firms/employer to the employees in return for the services provided by the employee.
